<!-- GENERATED by tools/gen-docs.js from js/labels.js — do not edit by hand. -->
# Grammar Lab — label reference

Every label the app supports, in the same words it uses on screen. Use this to
decide **which label you mean**; the `id` in each row is what you write when you
build a lesson file by hand or with a custom GPT.

- **Layers.** Four teaching levels, smallest unit first. A lesson's `layers`
  array holds the layer ids below.
- **Drill-down.** Every layer has base labels that can be applied on their own or
  opened to a more specific subtype. A subtype behaves exactly like its base —
  same color family, same quiz treatment.
- **⚑ Advanced.** Four labels sit outside the classroom-core set and are hidden
  by a lesson's **Essential only** toggle. Everything else is Essential.

| Layer | id | Unit | Labels |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parts of Speech | `pos` | word | 9 base + 45 subtypes = **54** |
| Sentence Parts | `part` | group of words | 5 base + 14 subtypes = **19** |
| Phrases | `phrase` | phrase | 6 base + 3 subtypes = **9** |
| Clauses | `clause` | clause | 2 base + 3 subtypes = **5** |

**Totals:** 87 span labels (83 Essential, 4 Advanced) plus 8 sentence-type badges.

---

## Parts of Speech — layer id `pos`

Label individual words: noun, verb, adjective…

### Noun — `noun`

A person, place, thing, or idea.

> The **fox** crossed the **river**.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Common Noun | `common-noun` | Names a general person, place, thing, or idea — not capitalized. | The **city** by the **river**. |
| Proper Noun | `proper-noun` | Names a specific person, place, or thing — always capitalized. | **London** sits on the **Thames**. |
| Collective Noun | `collective-noun` | Names a group of people or things as a single unit. | The **team** celebrated. A **flock** of geese. |
| Abstract Noun | `abstract-noun` | Names an idea, quality, or feeling you cannot touch. | Her **courage** gave us **hope**. |
| Concrete Noun | `concrete-noun` | Names something you can perceive with your senses. | The **rain** soaked the **dog**. |
| Possessive Noun | `possessive-noun` | A noun showing ownership, formed with an apostrophe. | The **dog's** bone; the **players'** bench. |

### Verb — `verb`

An action or a state of being.

> She **runs**. He **is** tired.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Action Verb | `action-verb` | Shows a physical or mental action. | She **kicked** the ball and **thought** hard. |
| Linking Verb | `linking-verb` | Links the subject to a word that renames or describes it. | The river **is** frozen. She **seems** tired. |
| Helping (Auxiliary) Verb | `helping-verb` | Works with a main verb to build tense, voice, or mood. | She **has** waited. They **are** leaving. |
| Transitive Verb | `transitive-verb` | An action verb that passes its action to a direct object. | She **kicked** the ball. |
| Intransitive Verb | `intransitive-verb` | An action verb with no direct object. | The baby **smiled**. He **slept**. |
| Modal Verb | `modal-verb` | A helping verb expressing possibility, ability, or necessity. | You **can** go. She **must** stay. |
| Regular Verb | `regular-verb` | Forms its past tense by adding -ed. | She **walked** home and **waited**. |
| Irregular Verb | `irregular-verb` | Changes form in the past tense instead of adding -ed. | She **ran** home and **went** inside. |
| Gerund | `gerund` | An -ing verb form acting as a noun. | **Skating** is risky. She loves **reading**. |
| Participle | `participle` | A verb form acting as an adjective — usually ending in -ing or -ed. | The **frozen** river; the **howling** wind. |
| Infinitive | `infinitive` | “To” plus the base verb, acting as a noun, adjective, or adverb. | She wanted **to leave**. A book **to read**. |
| Particle (phrasal verb) ⚑ | `particle` | A short word that joins a verb to change its meaning — it looks like a preposition but has no object. | She looked **up** the word and gave **up**. |

### Adjective — `adjective`

A word that describes a noun or pronoun.

> The **quick**, **clever** fox.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Descriptive Adjective | `descriptive-adjective` | Names a quality of a noun — how it looks, feels, or seems. | A **tall**, **blue** door. |
| Proper Adjective | `proper-adjective` | An adjective formed from a proper noun — capitalized. | **French** bread; a **Shakespearean** play. |
| Demonstrative Adjective | `demonstrative-adjective` | Points to which one, right before a noun: this, that, these, those. | **This** book and **those** pens. |
| Possessive Adjective | `possessive-adjective` | Shows ownership before a noun: my, your, his, her, its, our, their. | **My** dog chased **their** ball. |
| Adjective of Quantity/Number | `quantitative-adjective` | Tells how much or how many. | **Three** apples and **many** friends. |
| Comparative Adjective | `comparative-adjective` | Compares two things — often ends in -er or uses “more.” | The path is **icier** and **more dangerous**. |
| Superlative Adjective | `superlative-adjective` | Compares three or more — often ends in -est or uses “most.” | The **iciest**, **most dangerous** path. |

### Adverb — `adverb`

A word that modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb.

> She moved **carefully** and **very** quietly.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adverb of Manner | `adverb-of-manner` | Tells how something happens — often ends in -ly. | She skated **gracefully**. |
| Adverb of Time | `adverb-of-time` | Tells when something happens. | They left **yesterday** and will return **soon**. |
| Adverb of Place | `adverb-of-place` | Tells where something happens. | Look **here**, not **there**. |
| Adverb of Frequency | `adverb-of-frequency` | Tells how often something happens. | She **always** wins; he **never** quits. |
| Adverb of Degree | `adverb-of-degree` | Tells how much or to what extent. | It was **very** cold and **quite** late. |
| Relative Adverb ⚑ | `relative-adverb` | Begins a clause that describes a noun: where, when, why. | The day **when** the ice broke; the reason **why** she stopped. |

### Pronoun — `pronoun`

A word that takes the place of a noun.

> **She** gave **it** to **them**.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Personal Pronoun | `personal-pronoun` | Stands for a specific person or thing: I, you, he, she, it, we, they. | **She** told **him** the news. |
| Possessive Pronoun | `possessive-pronoun` | Shows ownership and stands alone: mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs. | That book is **mine**, not **yours**. |
| Reflexive Pronoun | `reflexive-pronoun` | Refers back to the subject: myself, yourself, itself, themselves… | She taught **herself** to skate. |
| Relative Pronoun | `relative-pronoun` | Begins a relative clause: who, whom, whose, which, that. | The path **that** led home was icy. |
| Demonstrative Pronoun | `demonstrative-pronoun` | Points to something and stands alone: this, that, these, those. | **This** is mine; **those** are hers. |
| Interrogative Pronoun | `interrogative-pronoun` | Asks a question: who, whom, whose, which, what. | **Who** is there? **What** happened? |
| Indefinite Pronoun | `indefinite-pronoun` | Refers to no specific person or thing: anyone, everyone, some, none… | **Everyone** left; **nothing** remained. |
| Emphatic Pronoun ⚑ | `emphatic-pronoun` | Same form as a reflexive pronoun, but added only for emphasis — the sentence still works without it. | She **herself** crossed the ice. I built it **myself**. |

### Preposition — `preposition`

A word that shows a relationship of place, time, or direction.

> The cat hid **under** the porch **during** the storm.

### Conjunction — `conjunction`

A word that joins words, phrases, or clauses.

> Slow **but** steady, **and** always calm.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coordinating Conjunction | `coordinating-conjunction` | Joins equal words or clauses (FANBOYS): for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so. | Slow **but** steady; fast **and** loud. |
| Subordinating Conjunction | `subordinating-conjunction` | Begins a dependent clause: because, although, when, if, since… | **Because** the ice was thin, she stopped. |
| Correlative Conjunction | `correlative-conjunction` | Works in a pair: either/or, neither/nor, both/and, not only/but also. | **Either** stay **or** go. |

### Determiner — `determiner`

A word that introduces a noun: articles, demonstratives, quantifiers.

> **The** dog chased **a** ball past **those** trees.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Article | `article` | The most common determiners: a, an, and the. | **The** dog chased **a** ball. |
| Definite Article | `definite-article` | “The” — points to a specific noun the reader already knows. | **The** fox crossed **the** river. |
| Indefinite Article | `indefinite-article` | “A” or “an” — introduces a noun for the first time or in general. | **A** fox and **an** owl. |

### Interjection — `interjection`

A word that expresses sudden emotion.

> **Wow!** That was close. **Ouch!**

## Sentence Parts — layer id `part`

Label functional parts: subject, predicate, objects…

### Subject — `subject`

Who or what the sentence is about — the doer of the verb.

> **The curious fox** darted across the river.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Subject | `simple-subject` | The single main noun or pronoun in the subject, without its modifiers. | The curious little **fox** darted across the river. |
| Complete Subject | `complete-subject` | The simple subject plus all the words that describe it. | **The curious little fox** darted across the river. |
| Compound Subject | `compound-subject` | Two or more subjects that share the same verb, joined by a conjunction. | **The fox and the hare** raced downhill. |
| Understood Subject | `understood-subject` | The unstated “you” that is the subject of a command. Because it is not written, mark the word it commands and name the missing subject. | **Watch** the thin ice. — the subject “(you)” is understood. |

### Predicate — `predicate`

What the subject does or is — the verb and everything that goes with it.

> The fox **darted across the frozen river**.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Predicate | `simple-predicate` | The verb (or verb phrase) alone — the action or state, without its modifiers or objects. | The curious fox **darted** across the river. |
| Complete Predicate | `complete-predicate` | The verb plus all the words that complete or modify it. | The fox **darted across the frozen river**. |
| Compound Predicate | `compound-predicate` | Two or more verbs that share the same subject, joined by a conjunction. | The fox **raced downhill and leaped over the log**. |

### Object — `object`

A noun or pronoun that receives the action of a verb or is governed by a preposition.

> She kicked **the ball**. The keys are under **the mat**.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct Object | `direct-object` | The noun or pronoun that receives the action of the verb. | She kicked **the ball**. |
| Indirect Object | `indirect-object` | The noun or pronoun that tells to whom or for whom the action is done. | She passed **her brother** the ball. |
| Object of the Preposition | `object-of-preposition` | The noun or pronoun governed by a preposition — it completes the prepositional phrase. | The keys are under **the mat** by **the door**. |

### Complement — `complement`

A word or group that completes the meaning of the subject or object.

> The river was **frozen solid**. They elected her **captain**.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Subject Complement | `subject-complement` | Follows a linking verb and renames or describes the subject. Its two kinds are the predicate nominative and the predicate adjective. | The river **is a highway**. The ice **looks solid**. |
| Predicate Nominative | `predicate-nominative` | A subject complement that is a noun or pronoun — it renames the subject after a linking verb. | The fox is **a hunter**. That skater became **a champion**. |
| Predicate Adjective | `predicate-adjective` | A subject complement that is an adjective — it describes the subject after a linking verb. | The river is **frozen**. The night grew **cold**. |
| Object Complement ⚑ | `object-complement` | Follows a direct object and renames or describes it. | They elected her **captain**. The frost painted the field **white**. |

### Appositive — `appositive`

A noun or pronoun set beside another to rename it. (A longer renaming word group is an appositive phrase.)

> My brother **Sam** skated by. The poet **Frost** wrote it.

## Phrases — layer id `phrase`

Label multi-word phrases: noun phrase, prepositional phrase…

### Noun Phrase — `noun-phrase`

A noun plus its modifiers, acting together as a noun.

> **The curious little fox** watched us.

### Verb Phrase — `verb-phrase`

A main verb plus its helping verbs.

> She **has been waiting** all day.

### Prepositional Phrase — `prepositional-phrase`

A preposition plus its object, acting as an adjective or adverb.

> The keys are **under the mat** **by the door**.

### Verbal Phrase — `verbal-phrase`

A phrase built on a verb form — gerund, participle, or infinitive — that acts as a noun, adjective, or adverb rather than as the sentence's verb.

> **Skating on thin ice** is risky. She paused **to catch her breath**.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gerund Phrase | `gerund-phrase` | An -ing verb form plus its objects and modifiers, acting as a noun. | **Skating on thin ice** is risky. |
| Infinitive Phrase | `infinitive-phrase` | “To” plus a verb and its objects, acting as a noun, adjective, or adverb. | She paused **to catch her breath**. |
| Participial Phrase | `participial-phrase` | A participle plus its objects and modifiers, acting as an adjective. | **Startled by the noise**, the deer froze. |

### Appositive Phrase — `appositive-phrase`

A noun phrase that renames the noun right beside it.

> My neighbor, **a retired pilot**, tells great stories.

### Absolute Phrase — `absolute-phrase`

A noun plus a participle that modifies the whole sentence.

> **Her heart pounding**, she opened the door.

## Clauses — layer id `clause`

Label clauses: independent, dependent, relative…

### Independent Clause — `independent-clause`

A subject and verb that can stand alone as a complete sentence.

> **The ice held**, and **she crossed safely**.

### Dependent Clause — `dependent-clause`

A subject and verb that cannot stand alone — it depends on the rest of the sentence.

> **Because the ice was thin**, she moved carefully.

| Subtype | id | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relative (Adjective) Clause | `relative-clause` | A dependent clause that describes a noun, usually starting with who, which, or that. | The path **that led to the river** was icy. |
| Adverbial Clause | `adverbial-clause` | A dependent clause that tells when, where, why, or how. | **When the sun rose**, the ice began to melt. |
| Noun Clause | `noun-clause` | A dependent clause that acts as a noun. | She knew **that the river was dangerous**. |

---

## Sentence types

Set per sentence, on two independent axes — these are whole-sentence badges, not
span labels. In a lesson file they go in a sentence's optional `types` object.

### Structure — `types.structure`

How many clauses, and of what kind.

| Value | Name | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `simple` | Simple | One independent clause. (It may still have a compound subject or predicate.) | The fox crossed the river. |
| `compound` | Compound | Two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction or a semicolon. | The ice held, and she crossed safely. |
| `complex` | Complex | One independent clause plus at least one dependent clause. | Because the ice was thin, she moved carefully. |
| `compound-complex` | Compound-Complex | Two or more independent clauses and at least one dependent clause. | When the sun rose, the ice melted, and the fox turned back. |

### Purpose — `types.purpose`

What the sentence does — and how it ends.

| Value | Name | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `declarative` | Declarative | Makes a statement. Ends with a period. | The fox crossed the river. |
| `interrogative` | Interrogative | Asks a question. Ends with a question mark. | Did the fox cross the river? |
| `imperative` | Imperative | Gives a command or makes a request. The subject “you” is usually understood. | Cross the river carefully. |
| `exclamatory` | Exclamatory | Expresses strong emotion. Ends with an exclamation point. | What a leap that was! |

---
